title: NASA says July 2023 was Earth's hottest month ever on record
description: NASA and NOAA confirm that last month was the hottest July on Earth in recorded history.
bodyText: Last month was the hottest July in recorded history, according to data released by NASA and NOAA. If this summer felt hotter than ever before, it's because it was. Earth just had its hottest July in recorded history, according to data from NASA and the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration (NOAA). The month is officially now the hottest July on record since meteorological documentation began in 1880. The staggering milestone follows what was our planet's hottest June ever recorded. Together, NASA and NOAA found that the average global surface temperature in July was 2.02 degrees Fahrenheit above average. Last month was the first time an average July temperature exceeded 1.8 degrees above the long-term average, scientists said. July 2023 also marked the fourth-consecutive month the global ocean surface temperature hit a record high. Additionally, July set a record for the highest monthly sea surface temperature anomaly, recording a spike of 1.78 degrees over the long-term average. Article continues below this ad (1 of 5) IT’S OFFICIAL:Earth just had its hottest July in 174 years.4th consecutive month of record-high global ocean surface temperature.Antarctica #SeaIce coverage had a record-low in July for the 3rd consecutive month.https://t.co/lRgypbiSed@NOAANCEI #StateOfClimate pic.twitter.com/n1ud8u87x2 Scientists attributed July's record warmth to a long-term trend of human-driven climate change, which was also likely enhanced by the naturally occurring climate pattern called El Niño. The cyclic phenomena, which warms the tropical Pacific Ocean, causes an increase to global temperatures and alters weather conditions around the world. The above average temperatures have accounted for heat warnings across the United States and have been linked to hundreds of heat-related illnesses and death deaths. This map shows global temperature anomalies for July 2023 according to the GISTEMP analysis by NASA's Goddard Institute for Space Studies. Temperature anomalies reflect how July 2023 compared to the average July temperature from 1951-1980. "Climate change is impacting people and ecosystems around the world, and we expect many of these impacts to escalate with continued warming," said Katherine Calvin, chief scientist and senior climate advisor at NASA headquarters in Washington. Article continues below this ad Unfortunately, the heat is not expected to let up next year. Scientists said they expect to see the biggest impacts of El Niño in February, March, and April 2024. Gavin Schmidt, director of NASA's Goddard Institute for Space Studies, said during a virtual media briefing in July: "We're anticipating that not only is 2023 going to be exceptionally warm and possibly a record warm year, but we anticipate that 2024 will be warmer still."
